bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde (New York: Johnson Reprint Corporation; London: Johnson Reprint Company Limited, 1967), reprinting the Chaucer Society edition of 1881–1882.
bibliographic.title: A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde

entities.label: Johnson Reprint of Furnivall's Parallel-Text Print
notes: The CME XML includes Johnson's reprint statement, making this the strongest identified immediate print layer.
No production documentation was found proving whether U-M keyed directly from this physical copy or from an intermediate file.

rights.basis: The underlying 1881–1882 edition is public domain, but no copyright-registration, renewal, or new-matter analysis was completed for Johnson's 1967 reprint layer.
rights.component: The 1967 reprint as a whole, including any new matter, typography, or production elements
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: Do not treat uncertainty about the reprint's new matter as extending copyright in Chaucer or Furnivall's nineteenth-century edition.

bibliographic.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde from the Campsall MS. of Mr. Bacon Frank, the Harleian MS. 2280, and Cambridge University Library MS. Gg.4.27, Chaucer Society, First Series 63–64, 2 parts (London: N. Trübner, 1881–1882).
bibliographic.title: A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde

entities.label: Furnivall parallel-text edition, 1881–1882
notes: Part I contains Books I–II, printed pp. 1–102; Part II contains Books III–V, pp. 103–298.
The printed page presents the three transcriptions in adjacent columns, unlike the XML's sequential serialization.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1881–1882, well before the current U.S. public-domain cutoff described in Copyright Office Circular 15A.
rights.component: Furnivall's 1881–1882 edition text and original editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: This does not decide copyright or contractual restrictions in modern scans, later reprints, or manuscript photographs.

access.accessMethod: Morgan manuscript catalog record
access.entityLabel: Morgan Library & Museum MS M.817

access.accessMethod: Morgan public catalog and manuscript image viewer checked for complete coverage
access.entityLabel: Morgan Library & Museum MS M.817
access.format: No verified public complete-codex image set
notes: The public record exposes a selected fol. 1r image, not all 120 leaves.

access.accessMethod: Submit the Morgan Photo Services online application and request the complete physical manuscript rather than only the poem leaves
access.contact: Morgan Library & Museum Photo Services via the online application
access.entityLabel: Morgan Library & Museum MS M.817
access.format: Existing digital images or new photography, subject to condition, availability, quotation, and permissions
notes: Ask for complete codex coverage, including bindings, flyleaves, blank surfaces, and fol. 120; separately identify Troilus as current fols. 1r–approximately 119v.
The Morgan gives an 8–12 week processing estimate and says new photography is considered case by case.

rights.basis: The Morgan requires an application, charges image and publication fees, and restricts unauthorized duplication and dissemination.
rights.component: Any complete-codex images newly supplied by the Morgan
rights.jurisdiction: Morgan image-service terms; copyright jurisdiction not conclusively analyzed

dateStatements.label: production
dateStatements.notes: The Morgan describes it as made in England, probably London, within this securely datable range.

entities.description: The earliest securely datable witness of Troilus and Criseyde and the first column of Furnivall's edition.

holding.notes: Formerly at Campsall and sold from the F. Bacon Frank estate in 1942 before Morgan acquisition.

entities.label: Morgan Library & Museum MS M.817
notes: The Morgan calls it the only Chaucer manuscript certainly owned by the English royal family and associates it with the future Henry V; wording is kept short of asserting a proven royal commission.
Furnivall's historical leaf numbering starts at leaf 2 for the surface now labeled fol. 1r. The inferred constant offset through the ending remains to be checked against a complete surrogate.
Written in secretary with textura; decorated and armorial.
The poem begins on current Morgan fol. 1r. The final current folio is associated with ownership/provenance rather than the poem.

rights.basis: The Morgan requires an application, charges image and publication fees, and restricts unauthorized duplication and dissemination.
rights.component: Any complete-codex images newly supplied by the Morgan
rights.jurisdiction: Morgan image-service terms; copyright jurisdiction not conclusively analyzed

access.accessMethod: British Library Archives and Manuscripts catalog
access.entityLabel: British Library Harley MS 2280

access.accessMethod: British Library digital item viewer
access.entityLabel: British Library Harley MS 2280

access.accessMethod: Submit the British Library digitisation order form for photography of the complete physical manuscript
access.contact: British Library Imaging Services via the online order form
access.entityLabel: British Library Harley MS 2280
access.format: Photography of original manuscripts; exact complete-codex deliverables subject to quotation and condition
notes: Request fols. 1–98, all opening and closing flyleaves, bindings, blanks, and any stubs so the reproduction represents the whole physical object; map the work separately to fols. 1r–98r.
The service describes manuscript capture as photography rather than scanning; feasibility and price require a quote.

rights.basis: The service terms and rate card distinguish supply of images from permissions for reuse or publication.
rights.component: Any complete-codex photographs supplied by British Library Imaging Services
rights.jurisdiction: British Library imaging-service terms; copyright jurisdiction not conclusively analyzed

dateStatements.label: production
dateStatements.value: first half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A complete-text witness except for one lost leaf in Book V, printed in the second column of Furnivall's parallel edition.
entities.label: British Library Harley MS 2280
notes: The work occupies nearly the entire written codex, but a complete physical-object reproduction would also include flyleaves, bindings, and other non-textual surfaces.

physicalDescription.extent: 98 folios, with four paper and one parchment flyleaves at the beginning and six flyleaves at the end
notes: Written in England in Gothic script.
Troilus and Criseyde occupies fols. 1r–98r; one leaf lost between fols. 92 and 93 removes Book V lines 1345–1428.

rights.basis: The service terms and rate card distinguish supply of images from permissions for reuse or publication.
rights.component: Any complete-codex photographs supplied by British Library Imaging Services
rights.jurisdiction: British Library imaging-service terms; copyright jurisdiction not conclusively analyzed

access.accessMethod: CUL manuscript guides and public catalog routes checked for a whole-codex digital object
access.entityLabel: Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27
access.format: No verified public complete-codex image set

access.accessMethod: Request bespoke imaging through the Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory portal
access.entityLabel: Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27
access.format: Bespoke digital photography subject to condition, quotation, and licensing
notes: Request complete coverage of both presently bound parts, including bindings, flyleaves, blank and supplied leaves; map Furnivall's medieval Troilus transcription separately to fols. 14r–126v and the broad work region to fols. 14r–131v.
Fragile material may be restricted, and reproduction/publication permission is a separate question.

rights.basis: CUL offers bespoke imaging and licensing, with publication and permissions handled through its service process; no open license for requested manuscript images was found.
rights.component: Any complete-codex images supplied by the CUL Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory
rights.jurisdiction: Cambridge University Library service and licensing terms; copyright jurisdiction not conclusively analyzed

dateStatements.label: recent scholarly dating of the principal scribe

entities.description: A large Chaucer codex, now bound in two parts, whose fragmentary Troilus and Criseyde transcription forms the third column of Furnivall's edition.

holding.notes: The medieval codex and Elizabethan supplied leaves are now bound as two physical parts under the same shelfmark.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27
notes: Furnivall transcribes the extant medieval sequence beginning at his leaf 14 and does not silently substitute the later supplied opening.
The broad work region is fols. 14r–131v, with extensive losses; Furnivall's extant transcription ends at fol. 126v because fols. 127–131 are missing.

physicalDescription.extent: 517 leaves plus 36 later supplied leaves; 62 whole leaves reported missing
notes: The main medieval Troilus begins at fol. 14 and the Canterbury Tales begins at fol. 132.
The beginnings of all five books and other leaves are missing. Elizabethan replacement leaves are separately bound.

rights.basis: CUL offers bespoke imaging and licensing, with publication and permissions handled through its service process; no open license for requested manuscript images was found.
rights.component: Any complete-codex images supplied by the CUL Cultural Heritage Imaging Laboratory
rights.jurisdiction: Cambridge University Library service and licensing terms; copyright jurisdiction not conclusively analyzed

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections for item-level production metadata or scan/keying documentation.
Compare systematic defects in the XML against HathiTrust's Johnson volume and scans of the original parts.
openQuestions.question: Did U-M key CME00002 directly from a physical 1967 Johnson Reprint, or from an intermediate file or copy derived from that reprint?

next_steps: Request complete-codex quotations from the Morgan, British Library, and Cambridge rather than requesting only Troilus leaves.

For any supplied object, record the full file set and then map the work portion separately: Morgan current fols. 1r–approximately 119v; Harley fols. 1r–98r; Gg broad region fols. 14r–131v with Furnivall's extant medieval stream ending at 126v.
Recheck the British Library ARK periodically for restored digital images.
notes: The Morgan's selected fol. 1r image and the public CUL catalog PDF are not complete manuscript facsimiles.
The printed Gg facsimile may answer many visual questions, but it is not a verified public digital source and may not reproduce every physical-object surface.
openQuestions.question: Can a public or repository-supplied complete physical-object facsimile be obtained for Morgan M.817, Harley 2280, and Gg.4.27, including bindings, flyleaves, blanks, and both present Gg bindings?

next_steps: Verify the end of the work against a complete Morgan surrogate or repository foliation statement.
Preserve both historical and current locators in any future work-portion mapping.
openQuestions.question: Does Furnivall's historical leaf 120 correspond exactly to current Morgan fol. 119v, and where does the poem end relative to current fol. 120?

next_steps: Locate any fuller Furnivall convention statement in Chaucer Society paratexts or correspondence.
Compare representative italic spans against complete manuscript images, recording whether each reflects expansion, supplied text, correction, or another feature.
Do not expand the medium-confidence generalization into a locus-level claim without image evidence.
openQuestions.question: What exact manuscript features underlie each internal italic span in Furnivall's three transcriptions?

next_steps: Inspect every preliminary and terminal leaf of the HathiTrust volume for notices and added matter.
If reuse of Johnson-specific pages is planned, complete a registration and renewal analysis rather than relying only on the age of Furnivall's edition.
openQuestions.question: Does the 1967 Johnson Reprint contain copyrightable new matter or a still-relevant copyright notice or registration beyond the public-domain 1881–1882 edition?

reviewNotes: The poem, three manuscript objects, Furnivall's edition, Johnson's reprint, the U-M encoding, and the repository XML are modeled as distinct layers.
The manuscript access records distinguish a selected leaf, a full-codex facsimile, and a reproduction request; a selected image is never labeled complete.
Local caching records retrieval and fixity only. It does not authorize redistribution or publication of provider-supplied images.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a local copy of University of Michigan CME item CME00002. The item's title pages identify a 1967 Johnson Reprint of Frederick J. Furnivall's two-part 1881–1882 Chaucer Society parallel-text edition. Furnivall printed independent transcriptions of three manuscript witnesses in parallel columns: the Campsall manuscript, now Morgan MS M.817; British Library Harley MS 2280; and Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27. The XML transforms those columns into sequential witness-version divisions inside each of the poem's five books. No public complete digital facsimile of any of the three physical codices was verified on 2026-07-11: the Morgan exposes a selected opening image, the British Library reports its digital images unavailable, and no Cambridge whole-codex image service was found. Complete-codex imaging may be requested from each repository, and a three-volume printed facsimile of Gg.4.27 exists.

catalogSummary.author: Geoffrey Chaucer
catalogSummary.region: England; exact place of composition not securely established; narrative set in Troy
catalogSummary.title: Troilus and Criseyde

catalogingSubject.label: Troilus and Criseyde
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Geoffrey Chaucer's five-book Middle English poem, as represented in CME00002 by Frederick J. Furnivall's parallel transcriptions of the Campsall, Harley 2280, and Cambridge Gg.4.27 witnesses. The work is distinct from those three manuscript objects, Furnivall's 1881–1882 edition, its 1967 Johnson reprint, the U-M electronic encoding, and this repository's XML artifact. The XML serializes three witness versions within each book; those versions are representations of one work, not fifteen intrinsic chapters.

contentParts.label: Book I
notes: Metrics aggregate the three witness versions; Gg.4.27 lacks its opening two leaves and later leaves 28–29.
contentParts.summary: Troilus first sees and falls in love with Criseyde; Pandarus undertakes to mediate.

contentParts.label: Book II
notes: Metrics aggregate the three witness versions; Gg.4.27's opening is interrupted by removed leaves.
contentParts.summary: Pandarus advances the courtship and brings Troilus and Criseyde into communication.

contentParts.label: Book III
notes: Metrics aggregate the three witness versions; Gg.4.27 loses a leaf at the book boundary.
contentParts.summary: The lovers are united and experience the poem's central period of joy.

contentParts.label: Book IV
notes: Metrics aggregate the three witness versions; Gg.4.27 has major material and textual omissions in this book.
contentParts.summary: Criseyde is exchanged for Antenor, separating the lovers and provoking debate over destiny and free will.

contentParts.label: Book V
notes: Metrics aggregate the three witness versions. Harley 2280 loses one leaf (Book V, lines 1345–1428), and Gg.4.27 loses its ending with leaves 127–131.
contentParts.summary: Criseyde remains with the Greeks and Diomede; Troilus dies in battle and looks back on earthly love from the heavens.

assertion.notes: The Middle English Compendium's stencil gives c1385; the approximation is not normalized to an exact year.
This dates an extant copy, not composition.

scope.description: The earliest securely datable complete witness

extent.description: Five intrinsic books represented through fifteen witness-version divisions. The repository XML contains 3,410 LG elements and 23,862 L elements across all three transcriptions: Campsall 1,176/8,237; Harley 1,164/8,154; Gg.4.27 1,070/7,471. These are encoding metrics, not the stanza or line count of a single critical text.

formStatements.description: A five-book narrative poem predominantly in seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas (ababbcc), with embedded songs, letters, and prayers retained as verse.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Brief Latin incipits, explicits, names, and learned quotations do not make the poem as cataloged a substantively multilingual work.
The work record deliberately catalogs one poem, while the lineage record preserves three independent witness streams and their material gaps.
Furnivall's printed page arrangement is parallel; the XML's sequential DIV3 structure is an encoding transformation and must not be interpreted as the poem's intrinsic order.
No single dialect region is assigned to the abstract poem from this multi-witness source.

next_steps: Consult a current critical introduction devoted to the poem's dating and revision phases before adding normalized bounds.
openQuestions.question: Can the composition and revision chronology be narrowed beyond the conventional c. 1385 dating without overstating disputed internal and historical evidence?

next_steps: Use reading-level manuscript and editorial analysis; do not assign the work one source witness's LALME localization.
openQuestions.question: Which linguistic features belong to Chaucer's compositional language and which belong to the three scribes or Furnivall's transcription conventions?

notes: This does not assign a precise composition place or any one witness's scribal dialect to the abstract poem.

scope.description: Creative retelling drawing especially on Giovanni Boccaccio's Il Filostrato
The 1881–1882 parallel-text source edition represented by CME00002
Parallel diplomatic transcriptions of the Campsall, Harley 2280, and Gg.4.27 witnesses
Alignment, lineation, witness notes, and source-comparison marks in the parallel-text edition

summaries.value: A five-book Middle English narrative poem recounting Troilus's love for Criseyde during the Trojan War, their union through Pandarus, Criseyde's exchange to the Greek camp and relationship with Diomede, and Troilus's death and final vision beyond earthly mutability. Chaucer transforms Boccaccio's Il Filostrato through sustained reflection on love, agency, fidelity, Fortune, providence, and authorship.

CME00002 represents Furnivall's three-column edition as sequential XML divisions: for each of the five books, Campsall is version 1, Harley 2280 version 2, and Gg.4.27 version 3. Gg.4.27 is materially incomplete, and Harley 2280 lacks one leaf in Book V. Counts below therefore describe the encoding's witness streams, not one canonical text.

notes: A shortened transcription of the edition's long title. It names the parallel-text source layer rather than a medieval authorial title.
titles.value: A Parallel-Text Print of Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde from the Campsall MS. of Mr. Bacon Frank, the Harleian MS. 2280, and Cambridge University Library MS. Gg.4.27

notes: Used as the edition's opening/running heading; the XML miskeys one occurrence as ‘Chancer's Troilus.’

work.author: Geoffrey Chaucer

publication.embeddedTitle: A Parallel=Text Print OF Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde FROM THE CAMPSALL MS. OF MR. BACON FRANK, COPIED FOR HENRY V. WHEN PRINCE OF WALES, THE HARLEIAN MS. 2280 IN THE BRITISH MUSEUM, AND THE CAMBRIDGE UNIVERSITY LIBRARY MS. Gg. 4. 27.

Chaucer transforms Boccaccio's Il Filostrato through sustained reflection on love, agency, fidelity, Fortune, providence, and authorship.
